title: Hipparchus

infobox.value: c. 190 BC  [Nicaea](/wiki/Nicaea "Nicaea"), [Kingdom of Bithynia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Bithynia "Kingdom of Bithynia") (modern-day [İznik, Bursa](/wiki/%C4%B0znik,_Bursa "İznik, Bursa"), Turkey)
c. 120 BC (around age 70)  [Rhodes](/wiki/Rhodes "Rhodes"), [Roman Republic](/wiki/Roman_Republic "Roman Republic") (modern-day Greece)

([/hɪˈpɑːrkəs/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); [Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek"): [Ἵππαρχος](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E1%BC%BD%CF%80%CF%80%CE%B1%CF%81%CF%87%CE%BF%CF%82 "wikt:Ἵππαρχος"), *Hípparkhos*; c. 190 – c. 120 BC) was a [Greek astronomer](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_astronomy "Ancient Greek astronomy"), [geographer](/wiki/Geographer "Geographer"), and [mathematician](/wiki/Mathematician "Mathematician"). He is considered the founder of [trigonometry](/wiki/Trigonometry "Trigonometry"),[[1]](#cite_note-linton2004-2) but is most famous for his incidental discovery of the [precession of the equinoxes](/wiki/Precession_of_the_equinoxes "Precession of the equinoxes").[[2]](#cite_note-toomer1996-3) Hipparchus was born in [Nicaea](/wiki/Nicaea "Nicaea"), [Bithynia](/wiki/Bithynia "Bithynia"), and probably died on the island of [Rhodes](/wiki/Rhodes "Rhodes"), Greece. He is known to have been a working astronomer between 162 and 127 BC.[[3]](#cite_note-mccluskey2000-4)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Greek astronomer, geographer and mathematician (c. 190 – c. 120 BCE)

This article is about the Greek astronomer. For other uses, see [Hipparchus (disambiguation)](/wiki/Hipparchus_(disambiguation) "Hipparchus (disambiguation)").

**Hipparchus** ([/hɪˈpɑːrkəs/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English"); [Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek"): [Ἵππαρχος](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E1%BC%BD%CF%80%CF%80%CE%B1%CF%81%CF%87%CE%BF%CF%82 "wikt:Ἵππαρχος"), *Hípparkhos*; c. 190 – c. 120 BC) was a [Greek astronomer](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_astronomy "Ancient Greek astronomy"), [geographer](/wiki/Geographer "Geographer"), and [mathematician](/wiki/Mathematician "Mathematician"). He is considered the founder of [trigonometry](/wiki/Trigonometry "Trigonometry"),[[1]](#cite_note-linton2004-2) but is most famous for his incidental discovery of the [precession of the equinoxes](/wiki/Precession_of_the_equinoxes "Precession of the equinoxes").[[2]](#cite_note-toomer1996-3) Hipparchus was born in [Nicaea](/wiki/Nicaea "Nicaea"), [Bithynia](/wiki/Bithynia "Bithynia"), and probably died on the island of [Rhodes](/wiki/Rhodes "Rhodes"), Greece. He is known to have been a working astronomer between 162 and 127 BC.[[3]](#cite_note-mccluskey2000-4)

Hipparchus is considered the greatest ancient astronomical observer and, by some, the greatest overall astronomer of [antiquity](/wiki/Classical_antiquity "Classical antiquity").[[4]](#cite_note-willard1854-5)[[5]](#cite_note-6) He was the first whose quantitative and accurate models for the motion of the [Sun](/wiki/Sun "Sun") and [Moon](/wiki/Moon "Moon") survive. For this he certainly made use of the observations and perhaps the mathematical techniques accumulated over centuries by the [Babylonians](/wiki/Babylonia "Babylonia") and by [Meton of Athens](/wiki/Meton_of_Athens "Meton of Athens") (fifth century BC), [Timocharis](/wiki/Timocharis "Timocharis"), [Aristyllus](/wiki/Aristyllus "Aristyllus"), [Aristarchus of Samos](/wiki/Aristarchus_of_Samos "Aristarchus of Samos"), and [Eratosthenes](/wiki/Eratosthenes "Eratosthenes"), among others.[[6]](#cite_note-jones2017-7)

He developed trigonometry and constructed [trigonometric tables](/wiki/Trigonometric_tables "Trigonometric tables"), and he solved several problems of [spherical trigonometry](/wiki/Spherical_trigonometry "Spherical trigonometry"). His other reputed achievements include the discovery and measurement of Earth's precession, the compilation of the first known comprehensive [star catalog](/wiki/Star_catalog "Star catalog") from the western world, and possibly the invention of the [astrolabe](/wiki/Astrolabe "Astrolabe"), as well as of the [armillary sphere](/wiki/Armillary_sphere "Armillary sphere") that he may have used in creating the star catalogue. He contributed to [optics](/wiki/Optics "Optics"), developing an [atomist](/wiki/Atomism "Atomism") theory of light. He is often called the "father of astronomy",[[7]](#cite_note-newcomb1878-8)[[8]](#cite_note-glashan1895-9) a title conferred on him by [Jean Baptiste Joseph Delambre](/wiki/Jean_Baptiste_Joseph_Delambre "Jean Baptiste Joseph Delambre") in 1817.[[9]](#cite_note-delambre1817-10)

## Life and work

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hipparchus&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Life and work")]

Hipparchus was born in Nicaea ([Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_language "Ancient Greek language"): Νίκαια), in [Bithynia](/wiki/Bithynia "Bithynia"). The exact dates of his life are not known, but [Ptolemy](/wiki/Ptolemy "Ptolemy") attributes astronomical observations to him in the period from 147 to 127 BC, and some of these are stated as made in [Rhodes](/wiki/Rhodes "Rhodes"); earlier observations since 162 BC might also have been made by him. His birth date (c. 190 BC) was calculated by [Delambre](/wiki/Jean_Baptiste_Joseph_Delambre "Jean Baptiste Joseph Delambre") based on clues in his work. Hipparchus must have lived some time after 127 BC because he analyzed and published his observations from that year. Hipparchus obtained information from [Alexandria](/wiki/Alexandria "Alexandria") as well as [Babylon](/wiki/Babylon "Babylon"), but it is not known when or if he visited these places. He is believed to have died on the island of Rhodes, where he seems to have spent most of his later life.

In the second and third centuries, [coins](/wiki/Coin "Coin") were made in his honour in Bithynia that bear his name and show him with a [globe](/wiki/Globe "Globe").[[10]](#cite_note-11)

Relatively little of Hipparchus's direct work survives into modern times. Although he wrote at least fourteen books, only his commentary on the popular astronomical poem by [Aratus](/wiki/Aratus "Aratus") was preserved by later copyists. Most of what is known about Hipparchus comes from [Strabo](/wiki/Strabo "Strabo")'s *[Geography](/wiki/Geographica "Geographica")* and [Pliny](/wiki/Pliny_the_Elder "Pliny the Elder")'s *[Natural History](/wiki/Natural_History_(Pliny) "Natural History (Pliny)")* in the first century; Ptolemy's second-century *[Almagest](/wiki/Almagest "Almagest")*; and additional references to him in the fourth century by [Pappus](/wiki/Pappus_of_Alexandria "Pappus of Alexandria") and [Theon of Alexandria](/wiki/Theon_of_Alexandria "Theon of Alexandria") in their commentaries on the *Almagest*.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEToomer1978-12)[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJones2001-13)

Hipparchus's only preserved work is *Commentary on the Phaenomena of Eudoxus and Aratus* ([Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_language "Ancient Greek language"): Τῶν Ἀράτου καὶ Εὐδόξου φαινομένων ἐξήγησις). This is a highly critical commentary in the form of two books on a popular [poem](/wiki/Poem "Poem") by [Aratus](/wiki/Aratus "Aratus") based on the work by [Eudoxus](/wiki/Eudoxus_of_Cnidus "Eudoxus of Cnidus").[[13]](#cite_note-14) Hipparchus also made a list of his major works that apparently mentioned about fourteen books, but which is only known from references by later authors. His famous star catalog was incorporated into the one by Ptolemy and may be almost perfectly reconstructed by subtraction of two and two-thirds degrees from the longitudes of Ptolemy's stars [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*][*[dubious](/wiki/Wikipedia:Accuracy_dispute#Disputed_statement "Wikipedia:Accuracy dispute") – [discuss](/wiki/Talk:Hipparchus#Dubious "Talk:Hipparchus")*]. The first trigonometric table was apparently compiled by Hipparchus, who is consequently now known as "the father of trigonometry".

## Babylonian sources

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hipparchus&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Babylonian sources")]

Further information: [Babylonian astronomy](/wiki/Babylonian_astronomy "Babylonian astronomy")

Earlier Greek astronomers and mathematicians were influenced by Babylonian astronomy to some extent, for instance the period relations of the [Metonic cycle](/wiki/Metonic_cycle "Metonic cycle") and [Saros cycle](/wiki/Saros_cycle "Saros cycle") may have come from Babylonian sources (see "[Babylonian astronomical diaries](/wiki/Babylonian_astronomical_diaries "Babylonian astronomical diaries")"). Hipparchus seems to have been the first to exploit Babylonian astronomical knowledge and techniques systematically.[[14]](#cite_note-toomer1988-15) Eudoxus in the 4th century BC and [Timocharis](/wiki/Timocharis "Timocharis") and [Aristillus](/wiki/Aristillus "Aristillus") in the 3rd century BC already divided the ecliptic in 360 parts (our [degrees](/wiki/Degree_(angle) "Degree (angle)"), Greek: moira) of 60 [arcminutes](/wiki/Arcminutes "Arcminutes") and Hipparchus continued this tradition. It was only in Hipparchus's time (2nd century BC) when this division was introduced (probably by Hipparchus's contemporary [Hypsicles](/wiki/Hypsicles "Hypsicles")) for all circles in mathematics. [Eratosthenes](/wiki/Eratosthenes "Eratosthenes") (3rd century BC), in contrast, used a simpler [sexagesimal](/wiki/Sexagesimal "Sexagesimal") system dividing a circle into 60 parts. Hipparchus also adopted the Babylonian astronomical *[cubit](/wiki/Cubit "Cubit")* unit ([Akkadian](/wiki/Akkadian_language "Akkadian language") *ammatu*, Greek πῆχυς *pēchys*) that was equivalent to 2° or 2.5° ('large cubit').[[15]](#cite_note-bg1991-16)

Hipparchus probably compiled a list of Babylonian astronomical observations; [Gerald J. Toomer](/wiki/Gerald_J._Toomer "Gerald J. Toomer"), a historian of astronomy, has suggested that Ptolemy's knowledge of eclipse records and other Babylonian observations in the *Almagest* came from a list made by Hipparchus. Hipparchus's use of Babylonian sources has always been known in a general way, because of Ptolemy's statements, but the only text by Hipparchus that survives does not provide sufficient information to decide whether Hipparchus's knowledge (such as his usage of the units cubit and finger, degrees and minutes, or the concept of hour stars) was based on Babylonian practice.[[16]](#cite_note-hoffman-befunde-17) However, [Franz Xaver Kugler](/wiki/Franz_Xaver_Kugler "Franz Xaver Kugler") demonstrated that the synodic and anomalistic periods that Ptolemy attributes to Hipparchus had already been used in Babylonian [ephemerides](/wiki/Ephemerides "Ephemerides"), specifically the collection of texts nowadays called "System B" (sometimes attributed to [Kidinnu](/wiki/Kidinnu "Kidinnu")).[[17]](#cite_note-kugler1900-18)[*[pages needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*]

Hipparchus's long [draconitic](/wiki/Orbital_period "Orbital period") lunar period (5,458 months = 5,923 lunar nodal periods) also appears a few times in [Babylonian records](/wiki/Babylonian_astronomy "Babylonian astronomy").[[18]](#cite_note-aaboe1955-19) But the only such tablet explicitly dated is post-Hipparchus, so the direction of transmission is not settled by the tablets.

## Geometry, trigonometry and other mathematical techniques

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hipparchus&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Geometry, trigonometry and other mathematical techniques")]

Hipparchus was recognized as the first mathematician known to have possessed a [trigonometric table](/wiki/Trigonometric_table "Trigonometric table"), which he needed when computing the [eccentricity](/wiki/Eccentricity_(orbit) "Eccentricity (orbit)") of the [orbits](/wiki/Orbit "Orbit") of the Moon and Sun. He tabulated values for the [chord](/wiki/Chord_(geometry) "Chord (geometry)") function, which for a central angle in a circle gives the length of the straight line segment between the points where the angle intersects the circle. He may have computed this for a circle with a circumference of 21,600 units and a radius (rounded) of 3,438 units; this circle has a unit length for each arcminute along its perimeter. (This was “proven” by Toomer,[[19]](#cite_note-toomer1974-chordtable-20) but he later “cast doubt“ upon his earlier affirmation.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEToomer1984215-21) Other authors have argued that a circle of radius 3,600 units may instead have been used by Hipparchus.[[21]](#cite_note-klintberg2005-22)) He tabulated the chords for angles with increments of 7.5°. In modern terms, the chord subtended by a central angle in a circle of given radius R equals R times twice the [sine](/wiki/Sine "Sine") of half of the angle, i.e.:

:   ![{\displaystyle \operatorname {chord} \theta =2R\cdot \sin {\tfrac {1}{2}}\theta }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ff343d70bb09dd8ab5e3cd298dee101f32141e20)

The now-lost work in which Hipparchus is said to have developed his chord table, is called *Tōn en kuklōi eutheiōn* (*Of Lines Inside a Circle*) in [Theon of Alexandria](/wiki/Theon_of_Alexandria "Theon of Alexandria")'s fourth-century commentary on section I.10 of the *Almagest*. Some claim the table of Hipparchus may have survived in astronomical treatises in India, such as the *[Surya Siddhanta](/wiki/Surya_Siddhanta "Surya Siddhanta")*. Trigonometry was a significant innovation, because it allowed Greek astronomers to solve any triangle, and made it possible to make quantitative astronomical models and predictions using their preferred geometric techniques.[[19]](#cite_note-toomer1974-chordtable-20)

Hipparchus must have used a better approximation for [π](/wiki/Pi "Pi") than the one given by [Archimedes](/wiki/Archimedes "Archimedes") of between 3+10⁄71 (≈ 3.1408) and 3+1⁄7 (≈ 3.1429). Perhaps he had the approximation later used by Ptolemy, [sexagesimal](/wiki/Sexagesimal "Sexagesimal") 3;08,30 (≈ 3.1417) (*Almagest* VI.7).

Hipparchus could have constructed his chord table using the [Pythagorean theorem](/wiki/Pythagorean_theorem "Pythagorean theorem") and a theorem known to Archimedes. He also might have used the relationship between sides and diagonals of a [cyclic quadrilateral](/wiki/Cyclic_quadrilateral "Cyclic quadrilateral"), today called [Ptolemy's theorem](/wiki/Ptolemy%27s_theorem "Ptolemy's theorem") because its earliest extant source is a proof in the *Almagest* (I.10).

The [stereographic projection](/wiki/Stereographic_projection "Stereographic projection") was ambiguously attributed to Hipparchus by [Synesius](/wiki/Synesius "Synesius") (c. 400 AD), and on that basis Hipparchus is often credited with inventing it or at least knowing of it. However, some scholars believe this conclusion to be unjustified by available evidence.[[22]](#cite_note-synesius-23) The oldest extant description of the stereographic projection is found in [Ptolemy](/wiki/Ptolemy "Ptolemy")'s [*Planisphere*](/wiki/Planisphaerium "Planisphaerium") (2nd century AD).[[23]](#cite_note-neugebauer1949-24)

Besides geometry, Hipparchus also used [arithmetic](/wiki/Arithmetic "Arithmetic") techniques developed by the [Chaldeans](/wiki/Chaldea "Chaldea"). He was one of the first Greek mathematicians to do this and, in this way, expanded the techniques available to astronomers and geographers.

There are several indications that Hipparchus knew spherical trigonometry, but the first surviving text discussing it is by [Menelaus of Alexandria](/wiki/Menelaus_of_Alexandria "Menelaus of Alexandria") in the first century, who now, on that basis, commonly is credited with its discovery. (Previous to the finding of the proofs of Menelaus a century ago, Ptolemy was credited with the invention of spherical trigonometry.) Ptolemy later used spherical trigonometry to compute things such as the rising and setting points of the [ecliptic](/wiki/Ecliptic "Ecliptic"), or to take account of the lunar [parallax](/wiki/Parallax "Parallax"). If he did not use spherical trigonometry, Hipparchus may have used a globe for these tasks, reading values off coordinate grids drawn on it, or he may have made approximations from planar geometry, or perhaps used arithmetical approximations developed by the Chaldeans.

## Lunar and solar theory

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hipparchus&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Lunar and solar theory")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/HipparchusConstruction.svg/500px-HipparchusConstruction.svg.png)](/wiki/File:HipparchusConstruction.svg)

Geometric construction used by Hipparchus in his determination of the distances to the Sun and Moon

### Motion of the Moon

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hipparchus&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Motion of the Moon")]

Further information: [Lunar theory](/wiki/Lunar_theory "Lunar theory") and [Orbit of the Moon](/wiki/Orbit_of_the_Moon "Orbit of the Moon")

Hipparchus also studied the motion of the [Moon](/wiki/Moon "Moon") and confirmed the accurate values for two periods of its motion that Chaldean astronomers are widely presumed to have possessed before him. The traditional value (from Babylonian System B) for the mean [synodic month](/wiki/Synodic_month "Synodic month") is 29 days; 31,50,8,20 (sexagesimal) = 29.5305941... days. Expressed as 29 days + 12 hours + ⁠793/1080⁠ hours this value has been used later in the [Hebrew calendar](/wiki/Hebrew_calendar "Hebrew calendar"). The Chaldeans also knew that 251 [synodic months](/wiki/Synodic_month "Synodic month") ≈ 269 [anomalistic months](/wiki/Anomalistic_month "Anomalistic month"). Hipparchus used the multiple of this period by a factor of 17, because that interval is also an eclipse period, and is also close to an integer number of years (4,267 moons : 4,573 anomalistic periods : 4,630.53 nodal periods : 4,611.98 lunar orbits : 344.996 years : 344.982 solar orbits : 126,007.003 days : 126,351.985 rotations).[[b]](#cite_note-25) What was so exceptional and useful about the cycle was that all 345-year-interval eclipse pairs occur slightly more than 126,007 days apart within a tight range of only approximately ±1⁄2 hour, guaranteeing (after division by 4,267) an estimate of the synodic month correct to one part in order of magnitude 10 million.

Hipparchus could confirm his computations by comparing eclipses from his own time (presumably 27 January 141 BC and 26 November 139 BC according to Toomer[[24]](#cite_note-toomer1980-26)) with eclipses from Babylonian records 345 years earlier (*Almagest* IV.2[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJones2001-13)).

Later [al-Biruni](/wiki/Al-Biruni "Al-Biruni") (*Qanun* VII.2.II) and [Copernicus](/wiki/Nicolaus_Copernicus "Nicolaus Copernicus") (*de revolutionibus* IV.4) noted that the period of 4,267 moons is approximately five minutes longer than the value for the eclipse period that Ptolemy attributes to Hipparchus. However, the timing methods of the Babylonians had an error of no fewer than eight minutes.[[25]](#cite_note-sf1993-27)[[26]](#cite_note-ssm1997-28) Modern scholars agree that Hipparchus rounded the eclipse period to the nearest hour, and used it to confirm the validity of the traditional values, rather than to try to derive an improved value from his own observations. From modern ephemerides[[27]](#cite_note-ccf2002-29) and taking account of the change in the length of the day (see [ΔT](/wiki/%CE%94T_(timekeeping) "ΔT (timekeeping)")) we[*[who?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] estimate that the error in the assumed length of the synodic month was less than 0.2 second in the fourth century BC and less than 0.1 second in Hipparchus's time.

### Orbit of the Moon

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hipparchus&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Orbit of the Moon")]

It had been known for a long time that the motion of the Moon is not uniform: its speed varies. This is called its *anomaly* and it repeats with its own period; the [anomalistic month](/wiki/Anomalistic_month "Anomalistic month"). The Chaldeans took account of this arithmetically, and used a table giving the daily motion of the Moon according to the date within a long period. However, the Greeks preferred to think in geometrical models of the sky. At the end of the third century BC, [Apollonius of Perga](/wiki/Apollonius_of_Perga "Apollonius of Perga") had proposed two models for lunar and planetary motion:

1. In the first, the Moon would move uniformly along a circle, but the Earth would be eccentric, i.e., at some distance of the center of the circle. So the apparent angular speed of the Moon (and its distance) would vary.
2. The Moon would move uniformly (with some mean motion in anomaly) on a secondary circular orbit, called an *epicycle* that would move uniformly (with some mean motion in longitude) over the main circular orbit around the Earth, called *deferent*; see [deferent and epicycle](/wiki/Deferent_and_epicycle "Deferent and epicycle").

Apollonius demonstrated that these two models were in fact mathematically equivalent. However, all this was theory and had not been put to practice. Hipparchus is the first astronomer known to attempt to determine the relative proportions and actual sizes of these orbits. Hipparchus devised a geometrical method to find the parameters from three positions of the Moon at particular phases of its anomaly. In fact, he did this separately for the eccentric and the epicycle model. Ptolemy describes the details in the *Almagest* IV.11. Hipparchus used two sets of three lunar eclipse observations that he carefully selected to satisfy the requirements. The eccentric model he fitted to these eclipses from his Babylonian eclipse list: 22/23 December 383 BC, 18/19 June 382 BC, and 12/13 December 382 BC. The epicycle model he fitted to lunar eclipse observations made in Alexandria at 22 September 201 BC, 19 March 200 BC, and 11 September 200 BC.

* For the eccentric model, Hipparchus found for the ratio between the radius of the [eccenter](/w/index.php?title=Eccenter&action=edit&redlink=1 "Eccenter (page does not exist)") and the distance between the center of the eccenter and the center of the ecliptic (i.e., the observer on Earth): 3144 : 327+2⁄3;
* and for the epicycle model, the ratio between the radius of the deferent and the epicycle: 3122+1⁄2 : 247+1⁄2 .

These figures are due to the cumbersome unit he used in his chord table and may partly be due to some sloppy rounding and calculation errors by Hipparchus, for which Ptolemy criticised him while also making rounding errors. A simpler alternate reconstruction[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThurston2002-30) agrees with all four numbers. Hipparchus found inconsistent results; he later used the ratio of the epicycle model (3122+1⁄2 : 247+1⁄2), which is too small (60 : 4;45 sexagesimal). Ptolemy established a ratio of 60 : 5+1⁄4.[[29]](#cite_note-toomer1968-31) (The maximum angular deviation producible by this geometry is the arcsin of 5+1⁄4 divided by 60, or approximately 5° 1′, five degrees and one [arc minute](/wiki/Minute_and_second_of_arc "Minute and second of arc"), a figure that is sometimes therefore quoted as the equivalent of the Moon's [equation of the center](/wiki/Equation_of_the_center "Equation of the center") in the Hipparchan model.)

### Apparent motion of the Sun

Before Hipparchus, [Meton](/wiki/Meton "Meton"), [Euctemon](/wiki/Euctemon "Euctemon"), and their pupils at [Athens](/wiki/Athens "Athens") had made a solstice observation (i.e., timed the moment of the summer [solstice](/wiki/Solstice "Solstice")) on 27 June 432 BC ([proleptic Julian calendar](/wiki/Proleptic_Julian_calendar "Proleptic Julian calendar")). [Aristarchus of Samos](/wiki/Aristarchus_of_Samos "Aristarchus of Samos") is said to have done so in 280 BC, and Hipparchus also had an observation by [Archimedes](/wiki/Archimedes "Archimedes"). He observed the summer solstices in 146 and 135 BC both accurately to a few hours, but observations of the moment of [equinox](/wiki/Equinox "Equinox") were simpler, and he made twenty during his lifetime. Ptolemy gives an extensive discussion of Hipparchus's work on the length of the year in the *Almagest* III.1, and quotes many observations that Hipparchus made or used, spanning 162–128 BC, including an equinox timing by Hipparchus (at 24 March 146 BC at dawn) that differs by 5 hours from the observation made on [Alexandria](/wiki/Alexandria "Alexandria")'s large public [equatorial ring](/wiki/Equatorial_ring "Equatorial ring") that same day (at 1 hour before noon). Ptolemy claims his solar observations were on a transit instrument set in the meridian.

At the end of his career, Hipparchus wrote a book entitled *Peri eniausíou megéthous* ("On the Length of the Year") regarding his results. The established value for the [tropical year](/wiki/Tropical_year "Tropical year"), introduced by [Callippus](/wiki/Callippus "Callippus") in or before 330 BC was 365+1⁄4 days.[[30]](#cite_note-leverington2003-32) Speculating a Babylonian origin for the Callippic year is difficult to defend, since Babylon did not observe solstices thus the only extant System B year length was based on Greek solstices (see below). Hipparchus's equinox observations gave varying results, but he points out (quoted in *Almagest* III.1(H195)) that the observation errors by him and his predecessors may have been as large as 1⁄4 day. He used old solstice observations and determined a difference of approximately one day in approximately 300 years. So he set the length of the tropical year to 365+1⁄4 − 1⁄300 days (= 365.24666... days = 365 days 5 hours 55 min, which differs from the modern estimate of the value (including earth spin acceleration), in his time of approximately 365.2425 days, an error of approximately 6 min per year, an hour per decade, and ten hours per century.

Between the solstice observation of Meton and his own, there were 297 years spanning 108,478 days; this implies a tropical year of 365.24579... days = 365 days;14,44,51 (sexagesimal; = 365 days + ⁠14/60⁠ + ⁠44/602⁠ + ⁠51/603⁠), a year length found on one of the few Babylonian clay tablets which explicitly specifies the System B month. Whether Babylonians knew of Hipparchus's work or the other way around is debatable.

Hipparchus also gave the value for the [sidereal year](/wiki/Sidereal_year "Sidereal year") to be 365 + ⁠1/4⁠ + ⁠1/144⁠ days (= 365.25694... days = 365 days 6 hours 10 min). Another value for the sidereal year that is attributed to Hipparchus (by the physician [Galen](/wiki/Galen "Galen") in the second century AD) is 365 + ⁠1/4⁠ + ⁠1/288⁠ days (= 365.25347... days = 365 days 6 hours 5 min), but this may be a corruption of another value attributed to a Babylonian source: 365 + ⁠1/4⁠ + ⁠1/144⁠ days (= 365.25694... days = 365 days 6 hours 10 min). It is not clear whether Hipparchus got the value from Babylonian astronomers or calculated by himself.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENeugebauer1975Vol._1,_pp._293,_294-33)

### Orbit of the Sun

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hipparchus&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Orbit of the Sun")]

Before Hipparchus, astronomers knew that the lengths of the [seasons](/wiki/Season "Season") are not equal. Hipparchus made observations of equinox and solstice, and according to Ptolemy (*Almagest* III.4) determined that spring (from spring equinox to summer solstice) lasted 941⁄2 days, and summer (from summer solstice to autumn equinox) 92+1⁄2 days. This is inconsistent with a premise of the Sun moving around the Earth in a circle at uniform speed. Hipparchus's solution was to place the Earth not at the center of the Sun's motion, but at some distance from the center. This model described the apparent motion of the Sun fairly well. It is known today that the [planets](/wiki/Planet "Planet"), including the Earth, move in approximate [ellipses](/wiki/Ellipse "Ellipse") around the Sun, but this was not discovered until [Johannes Kepler](/wiki/Johannes_Kepler "Johannes Kepler") published his first two laws of planetary motion in 1609. The value for the [eccentricity](/wiki/Eccentricity_(orbit) "Eccentricity (orbit)") attributed to Hipparchus by Ptolemy is that the offset is 1⁄24 of the radius of the orbit (which is a little too large), and the direction of the [apogee](/wiki/Apogee "Apogee") would be at longitude 65.5° from the [vernal equinox](/wiki/March_equinox "March equinox"). Hipparchus may also have used other sets of observations, which would lead to different values. One of his two eclipse trios' solar longitudes are consistent with his having initially adopted inaccurate lengths for spring and summer of 95+3⁄4 and 91+1⁄4 days.[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThurston2002p._67,_note_16-34)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] His other triplet of solar positions is consistent with 94+1⁄4 and 92+1⁄2 days,[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJones2001-13)[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEThurston2002note_14-35)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] an improvement on the results (94+1⁄2 and 92+1⁄2 days) attributed to Hipparchus by Ptolemy. Ptolemy made no change three centuries later, and expressed lengths for the autumn and winter seasons which were already implicit (as shown, e.g., by A. [Aaboe](/wiki/Aaboe "Aaboe")).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Distance, parallax, size of the Moon and the Sun

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hipparchus&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Distance, parallax, size of the Moon and the Sun")]

Main article: [On Sizes and Distances (Hipparchus)](/wiki/On_Sizes_and_Distances_(Hipparchus) "On Sizes and Distances (Hipparchus)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/3/32/HipparchusEclipse.png)](/wiki/File:HipparchusEclipse.png)

Diagram used in reconstructing one of Hipparchus's methods of determining the distance to the Moon. This represents the Earth–Moon system during a partial solar eclipse at A ([Alexandria](/wiki/Alexandria "Alexandria")) and a total solar eclipse at H ([Hellespont](/wiki/Hellespont "Hellespont")).

Hipparchus also undertook to find the distances and sizes of the Sun and the Moon, in the now-lost work *On Sizes and Distances* ([Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_language "Ancient Greek language"): Περὶ μεγεθῶν καὶ ἀποστημάτων *Peri megethon kai apostematon*). His work is mentioned in Ptolemy's *Almagest* V.11, and in a commentary thereon by [Pappus](/wiki/Pappus_of_Alexandria "Pappus of Alexandria"); [Theon of Smyrna](/wiki/Theon_of_Smyrna "Theon of Smyrna") (2nd century) also mentions the work, under the title *On Sizes and Distances of the Sun and Moon*.

Hipparchus measured the apparent diameters of the Sun and Moon with his *[diopter](/wiki/Alidade "Alidade")*. Like others before and after him, he found that the Moon's size varies as it moves on its (eccentric) orbit, but he found no perceptible variation in the apparent diameter of the Sun. He found that at the *[mean](/wiki/Mean "Mean")* distance of the Moon, the Sun and Moon had the same apparent diameter; at that distance, the Moon's diameter fits 650 times into the circle, i.e., the mean apparent diameters are 360⁄650 = 0°33′14″.

Like others before and after him, he also noticed that the Moon has a noticeable [parallax](/wiki/Lunar_parallax "Lunar parallax"), i.e., that it appears displaced from its calculated position (compared to the Sun or [stars](/wiki/Star "Star")), and the difference is greater when closer to the horizon. He knew that this is because in the then-current models the Moon circles the center of the Earth, but the observer is at the surface—the Moon, Earth and observer form a triangle with a sharp angle that changes all the time. From the size of this parallax, the distance of the Moon as measured in Earth [radii](/wiki/Radius "Radius") can be determined. For the Sun however, there was no observable parallax (we now know that it is about 8.8″, eight point eight [arc seconds](/wiki/Minute_and_second_of_arc "Minute and second of arc"), several times smaller than the resolution of the unaided eye).

In the first book, Hipparchus assumes that the parallax of the Sun is 0, as if it is at infinite distance. He then analyzed a solar eclipse, which Toomer presumes to be the eclipse of 14 March 190 BC.[[34]](#cite_note-36) It was total in the region of the [Hellespont](/wiki/Hellespont "Hellespont") (and in his birthplace, Nicaea); at the time Toomer proposes the Romans were preparing for war with [Antiochus III](/wiki/Antiochus_III "Antiochus III") in the area, and the eclipse is mentioned by [Livy](/wiki/Livy "Livy") in his *[Ab Urbe Condita Libri](/wiki/Ab_Urbe_Condita_Libri "Ab Urbe Condita Libri")* VIII.2. It was also observed in Alexandria, where the Sun was reported to be obscured 4/5ths by the Moon. Alexandria and Nicaea are on the same meridian. Alexandria is at about 31° North, and the region of the Hellespont about 40° North. (It has been contended that authors like Strabo and Ptolemy had fairly decent values for these geographical positions, so Hipparchus must have known them too. However, Strabo's Hipparchus dependent latitudes for this region are at least 1° too high, and Ptolemy appears to copy them, placing Byzantium 2° high in latitude.) Hipparchus could draw a triangle formed by the two places and the Moon, and from simple geometry was able to establish a distance of the Moon, expressed in Earth radii. Because the eclipse occurred in the morning, the Moon was not in the [meridian](/wiki/Meridian_(astronomy) "Meridian (astronomy)"), and it has been proposed that as a consequence the distance found by Hipparchus was a lower limit. In any case, according to Pappus, Hipparchus found that the least distance is 71 (from this eclipse), and the greatest 83 Earth radii.

In the second book, Hipparchus starts from the opposite extreme assumption: he assigns a (minimum) distance to the Sun of 490 Earth radii. This would correspond to a parallax of 7′, which is apparently the greatest parallax that Hipparchus thought would not be noticed (for comparison: the typical resolution of the human eye is about 2′; [Tycho Brahe](/wiki/Tycho_Brahe "Tycho Brahe") made naked eye observation with an accuracy down to 1′). In this case, the shadow of the Earth is a [cone](/wiki/Conical_surface "Conical surface") rather than a [cylinder](/wiki/Cylinder_(geometry) "Cylinder (geometry)") as under the first assumption. Hipparchus observed (at lunar eclipses) that at the mean distance of the Moon, the diameter of the shadow cone is 2+1⁄2 lunar diameters. That apparent diameter is, as he had observed, 360⁄650 degrees. With these values and simple geometry, Hipparchus could determine the mean distance; because it was computed for a minimum distance of the Sun, it is the maximum mean distance possible for the Moon. With his value for the eccentricity of the orbit, he could compute the least and greatest distances of the Moon too. According to Pappus, he found a least distance of 62, a mean of 67+1⁄3, and consequently a greatest distance of 72+2⁄3 Earth radii. With this method, as the parallax of the Sun decreases (i.e., its distance increases), the minimum limit for the mean distance is 59 Earth radii—exactly the mean distance that Ptolemy later derived.

Hipparchus thus had the problematic result that his minimum distance (from book 1) was greater than his maximum mean distance (from book 2). He was intellectually honest about this discrepancy, and probably realized that especially the first method is very sensitive to the accuracy of the observations and parameters. (In fact, modern calculations show that the size of the 14.03.190 BC solar eclipse at Alexandria must have been closer to 9⁄10ths and not the reported 4⁄5ths, a fraction more closely matched by the degree of totality at Alexandria of eclipses occurring on 15.08.310 and 20.11.129 BC which were also nearly total in the Hellespont and are thought by many to be more likely possibilities for the eclipse Hipparchus used for his computations.)

Ptolemy later measured the lunar parallax directly (*Almagest* V.13), and used the second method of Hipparchus with lunar eclipses to compute the distance of the Sun (*Almagest* V.15). He criticizes Hipparchus for making contradictory assumptions, and obtaining conflicting results (*Almagest* V.11): but apparently he failed to understand Hipparchus's strategy to establish limits consistent with the observations, rather than a single value for the distance. His results were the best so far: the actual mean distance of the Moon is 60.3 Earth radii, within his limits from Hipparchus's second book.

[Theon of Smyrna](/wiki/Theon_of_Smyrna "Theon of Smyrna") wrote that according to Hipparchus, the Sun is 1,880 times the size of the Earth, and the Earth twenty-seven times the size of the Moon; apparently this refers to [volumes](/wiki/Volume "Volume"), not [diameters](/wiki/Diameter "Diameter"). From the geometry of book 2 it follows that the Sun is at 2,550 Earth radii, and the mean distance of the Moon is 60+1⁄2 radii. Similarly, [Cleomedes](/wiki/Cleomedes "Cleomedes") quotes Hipparchus for the sizes of the Sun and Earth as 1050:1; this leads to a mean lunar distance of 61 radii. Apparently Hipparchus later refined his computations, and derived accurate single values that he could use for predictions of solar eclipses.

See Toomer (1974) for a more detailed discussion.[[35]](#cite_note-toomer1974-sunmoon-37)

### Eclipses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hipparchus&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Eclipses")]

[Pliny](/wiki/Pliny_the_Elder "Pliny the Elder") (*Naturalis Historia* II.X) tells us that Hipparchus demonstrated that lunar eclipses can occur five months apart, and solar eclipses seven months (instead of the usual six months); and the Sun can be hidden twice in thirty days, but as seen by different nations. Ptolemy discussed this a century later at length in *Almagest* VI.6. The geometry, and the limits of the positions of Sun and Moon when a solar or lunar eclipse is possible, are explained in *Almagest* VI.5. Hipparchus apparently made similar calculations. The result that two solar eclipses can occur one month apart is important, because this can not be based on observations: one is visible on the Northern Hemisphere and the other on the Southern Hemisphere—as Pliny indicates—and the latter was inaccessible to the Greek.

Prediction of a solar eclipse, i.e., exactly when and where it will be visible, requires a solid lunar theory and proper treatment of the lunar parallax. Hipparchus must have been the first to be able to do this. A rigorous treatment requires [spherical trigonometry](/wiki/Spherical_trigonometry "Spherical trigonometry"), thus those who remain certain that Hipparchus lacked it must speculate that he may have made do with planar approximations. He may have discussed these things in *Perí tēs katá plátos mēniaías tēs selēnēs kinēseōs* ("On the monthly motion of the Moon in latitude"), a work mentioned in the *[Suda](/wiki/Suda "Suda")*.

Pliny also remarks that "he also discovered for what exact reason, although the shadow causing the eclipse must from sunrise onward be below the earth, it happened once in the past that the Moon was eclipsed in the west while both luminaries were visible above the earth" (translation H. Rackham (1938), [Loeb Classical Library](/wiki/Loeb_Classical_Library "Loeb Classical Library") 330 p. 207). Toomer argued that this must refer to the large total lunar eclipse of 26 November 139 BC, when over a clean sea horizon as seen from Rhodes, the Moon was eclipsed in the northwest just after the Sun rose in the southeast.[[24]](#cite_note-toomer1980-26) This would be the second eclipse of the 345-year interval that Hipparchus used to verify the traditional Babylonian periods: this puts a late date to the development of Hipparchus's lunar theory. It is not known what "exact reason" Hipparchus found for seeing the Moon eclipsed while apparently it was not in exact [opposition](/wiki/Astronomical_opposition "Astronomical opposition") to the Sun. Parallax lowers the altitude of the luminaries; refraction raises them, and from a high point of view the horizon is lowered.

## Astronomical instruments and astrometry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hipparchus&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Astronomical instruments and astrometry")]

Hipparchus and his predecessors used various instruments for astronomical calculations and observations, such as the [gnomon](/wiki/Gnomon "Gnomon"), the [astrolabe](/wiki/Astrolabe "Astrolabe"), and the [armillary sphere](/wiki/Armillary_sphere "Armillary sphere").

Hipparchus is credited with the invention or improvement of several astronomical instruments, which were used for a long time for naked-eye observations. According to [Synesius](/wiki/Synesius "Synesius") of Ptolemais (4th century) he made the first *astrolabion*: this may have been an [armillary sphere](/wiki/Armillary_sphere "Armillary sphere") (which Ptolemy however says he constructed, in *Almagest* V.1); or the predecessor of the planar instrument called astrolabe (also mentioned by [Theon of Alexandria](/wiki/Theon_of_Alexandria "Theon of Alexandria")). With an astrolabe Hipparchus was the first to be able to measure the geographical [latitude](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude") and [time](/wiki/Time "Time") by observing fixed stars. Previously this was done at daytime by measuring the shadow cast by a gnomon, by recording the length of the longest day of the year or with the portable instrument known as a *[scaphe](/wiki/Scaphe "Scaphe")*.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/Equatorial_ring.png/250px-Equatorial_ring.png)](/wiki/File:Equatorial_ring.png)

[Equatorial ring](/wiki/Equatorial_ring "Equatorial ring") of Hipparchus's time.

Ptolemy mentions (*Almagest* V.14) that he used a similar instrument as Hipparchus, called *[dioptra](/wiki/Dioptra "Dioptra")*, to measure the apparent diameter of the Sun and Moon. [Pappus of Alexandria](/wiki/Pappus_of_Alexandria "Pappus of Alexandria") described it (in his commentary on the *Almagest* of that chapter), as did [Proclus](/wiki/Proclus "Proclus") (*Hypotyposis* IV). It was a four-foot rod with a scale, a sighting hole at one end, and a wedge that could be moved along the rod to exactly obscure the disk of Sun or Moon.

Hipparchus also observed solar [equinoxes](/wiki/Equinox "Equinox"), which may be done with an [equatorial ring](/wiki/Equatorial_ring "Equatorial ring"): its shadow falls on itself when the Sun is on the [equator](/wiki/Equator "Equator") (i.e., in one of the equinoctial points on the [ecliptic](/wiki/Ecliptic "Ecliptic")), but the shadow falls above or below the opposite side of the ring when the Sun is south or north of the equator. Ptolemy quotes (in *Almagest* III.1 (H195)) a description by Hipparchus of an equatorial ring in Alexandria; a little further he describes two such instruments present in Alexandria in his own time.

Hipparchus applied his knowledge of spherical angles to the problem of denoting locations on the Earth's surface. Before him a grid system had been used by [Dicaearchus](/wiki/Dicaearchus "Dicaearchus") of [Messana](/wiki/Messina,_Italy "Messina, Italy"), but Hipparchus was the first to apply mathematical rigor to the determination of the [latitude](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude") and [longitude](/wiki/Longitude "Longitude") of places on the Earth. Hipparchus wrote a critique in three books on the work of the geographer [Eratosthenes](/wiki/Eratosthenes "Eratosthenes") of Cyrene (3rd century BC), called *Pròs tèn Eratosthénous geographían* ("Against the Geography of Eratosthenes"). It is known to us from [Strabo](/wiki/Strabo "Strabo") of Amaseia, who in his turn criticised Hipparchus in his own *Geographia*. Hipparchus apparently made many detailed corrections to the locations and distances mentioned by Eratosthenes. It seems he did not introduce many improvements in methods, but he did propose a means to determine the [geographical longitudes](/wiki/Geographic_coordinate_system "Geographic coordinate system") of different [cities](/wiki/City "City") at [lunar eclipses](/wiki/Lunar_eclipse "Lunar eclipse") (Strabo *Geographia* 1 January 2012). A lunar eclipse is visible simultaneously on half of the Earth, and the difference in longitude between places can be computed from the difference in local time when the eclipse is observed. His approach would give accurate results if it were correctly carried out but the limitations of timekeeping accuracy in his era made this method impractical.

## Star catalog

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hipparchus&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Star catalog")]

Late in his career (possibly about 135 BC) Hipparchus compiled his star catalog. Scholars have been searching for it for centuries.[[36]](#cite_note-swerdlow1992-38) In 2022, it was announced that a part of it was discovered in a medieval parchment manuscript, [Codex Climaci Rescriptus](/wiki/Codex_Climaci_Rescriptus "Codex Climaci Rescriptus"), from [Saint Catherine's Monastery](/wiki/Saint_Catherine%27s_Monastery "Saint Catherine's Monastery") in the [Sinai Peninsula](/wiki/Sinai_Peninsula "Sinai Peninsula"), Egypt as hidden text ([palimpsest](/wiki/Palimpsest "Palimpsest")).[[37]](#cite_note-gwz2022-39)[[38]](#cite_note-40) This finding was questioned in 2024,[[39]](#cite_note-41) with a rebuttal by the discoverers in 2025.[[40]](#cite_note-42) There is ongoing scholarly debate about the interpretation of the discovery.[[41]](#cite_note-43)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/School_of_Athens_Raphael_detail_03.jpg/250px-School_of_Athens_Raphael_detail_03.jpg)](/wiki/File:School_of_Athens_Raphael_detail_03.jpg)

The figure on the left may be Hipparchus, from [Raphael](/wiki/Raphael "Raphael")’s fresco *[The School of Athens](/wiki/The_School_of_Athens "The School of Athens")*

Hipparchus also constructed a celestial globe depicting the constellations, based on his observations. His interest in the [fixed stars](/wiki/Fixed_star "Fixed star") may have been inspired by the observation of a [supernova](/wiki/Supernova "Supernova") (according to Pliny), or by his discovery of precession, according to Ptolemy, who says that Hipparchus could not reconcile his data with earlier observations made by [Timocharis](/wiki/Timocharis "Timocharis") and [Aristillus](/wiki/Aristillus "Aristillus"). For more information see [Discovery of precession](/wiki/Discovery_of_precession "Discovery of precession"). In [Raphael](/wiki/Raphael "Raphael")'s painting *[The School of Athens](/wiki/The_School_of_Athens "The School of Athens")*, Hipparchus may be depicted holding his celestial globe, as the representative figure for astronomy. It is not certain that the figure is meant to represent him.[[36]](#cite_note-swerdlow1992-38)

Previously, [Eudoxus of Cnidus](/wiki/Eudoxus_of_Cnidus "Eudoxus of Cnidus") in the fourth century BC had described the stars and constellations in two books called *Phaenomena* and *Entropon*. [Aratus](/wiki/Aratus "Aratus") wrote a poem called *Phaenomena* or *Arateia* based on Eudoxus's work. Hipparchus wrote a commentary on the *Arateia*—his only preserved work—which contains many stellar positions and times for rising, culmination, and setting of the constellations, and these are likely to have been based on his own measurements.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/19th_century_Hipparchus_engraving.jpg/250px-19th_century_Hipparchus_engraving.jpg)](/wiki/File:19th_century_Hipparchus_engraving.jpg)

A 19th century artist's impression of Hipparchus[[42]](#cite_note-ks-engraving-44)

According to Roman sources, Hipparchus made his measurements with a scientific instrument and he obtained the positions of roughly 850 stars. Pliny the Elder writes in book II, 24–26 of his Natural History:[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoffmann2017-45)

> This same Hipparchus, who can never be sufficiently commended, ... discovered a new star that was produced in his own age, and, by observing its motions on the day in which it shone, he was led to doubt whether it does not often happen, that those stars have motion which we suppose to be fixed. And the same individual attempted, what might seem presumptuous even in a deity, viz. to number the stars for posterity and to express their relations by appropriate names; having previously devised instruments, by which he might mark the places and the magnitudes of each individual star. In this way it might be easily discovered, not only whether they were destroyed or produced, but whether they changed their relative positions, and likewise, whether they were increased or diminished; the heavens being thus left as an inheritance to any one, who might be found competent to complete his plan.

This passage reports that

* Hipparchus was inspired by a newly emerging star
* he doubts on the stability of stellar brightnesses
* he observed with appropriate instruments (plural—it is not said that he observed everything with the same instrument)
* he made a catalogue of stars

It is unknown what instrument he used. The [armillary sphere](/wiki/Armillary_sphere "Armillary sphere") was probably invented only later—maybe by Ptolemy 265 years after Hipparchus. The historian of science S. Hoffmann found clues that Hipparchus may have observed the longitudes and latitudes in different coordinate systems and, thus, with different instrumentation.[[16]](#cite_note-hoffman-befunde-17) Right ascensions, for instance, could have been observed with a clock, while angular separations could have been measured with another device.

### Stellar magnitude

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hipparchus&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Stellar magnitude")]

Hipparchus is conjectured to have ranked the [apparent magnitudes](/wiki/Apparent_magnitude "Apparent magnitude") of stars on a numerical scale from 1, the brightest, to 6, the faintest.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEToomer1984[httpsarchiveorgdetailsptolemysalmagest0000ptolpage16_p. 16]:_"The_magnitudes_range_(according_to_a_system_which_certainly_precedes_Ptolemy,_but_is_only_conjecturally_attributed_to_Hipparchus)_from_1_to_6.",_pp. 341–399-46) This hypothesis is based on the vague statement by Pliny the Elder but cannot be proven by the data in Hipparchus's commentary on Aratus's poem. In this only work by his hand that has survived until today, he does not use the magnitude scale but estimates brightnesses unsystematically. However, this does not prove or disprove anything because the commentary might be an early work while the magnitude scale could have been introduced later.[[16]](#cite_note-hoffman-befunde-17) Yet, it was proven that the error bars of magnitudes in ancient star catalogue is 1.5 mag which suggests that these numbers are not based on measurements.[[45]](#cite_note-47) There were several suggestions on measurement methodologies and feasibility studies.[[46]](#cite_note-48) In all cases, the error bars would be smaller. Hence, Hoffmann (2022) suggested that the magnitudes were not measured at all but mere estimates for globe makers to improve pattern recognition on globes as astronomer's computing machines.[[47]](#cite_note-49)

Nevertheless, this system certainly precedes [Ptolemy](/wiki/Ptolemy "Ptolemy"), who used it extensively about AD 150.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEToomer1984[httpsarchiveorgdetailsptolemysalmagest0000ptolpage16_p. 16]:_"The_magnitudes_range_(according_to_a_system_which_certainly_precedes_Ptolemy,_but_is_only_conjecturally_attributed_to_Hipparchus)_from_1_to_6.",_pp. 341–399-46) This system was made more precise and extended by [N. R. Pogson](/wiki/N._R._Pogson "N. R. Pogson") in 1856, who placed the magnitudes on a logarithmic scale, making magnitude 1 stars 100 times brighter than magnitude 6 stars, thus each magnitude is 5√100 or 2.512 times brighter than the next faintest magnitude.[[48]](#cite_note-50)

### Coordinate system

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hipparchus&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Coordinate system")]

It is disputed which coordinate system(s) he used. Ptolemy's catalog in the *[Almagest](/wiki/Almagest "Almagest")*, which is derived from Hipparchus's catalog, is given in [ecliptic coordinates](/wiki/Ecliptic_coordinate_system "Ecliptic coordinate system"). Although Hipparchus strictly distinguishes between "signs" (30° section of the zodiac) and "constellations" in the zodiac, it is highly questionable whether or not he had an instrument to directly observe / measure units on the ecliptic.[[16]](#cite_note-hoffman-befunde-17)[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoffmann2017-45) He probably marked them as a unit on his celestial globe but the instrumentation for his observations is unknown.[[16]](#cite_note-hoffman-befunde-17)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/Alm_signs%2Bconsts.jpg/500px-Alm_signs%2Bconsts.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alm_signs%2Bconsts.jpg)

Ptolemy's constellation areas (blue polygons) and "signs" of the zodiac had different sizes and extends; it is highly likely Hipparchus considered these units the same. Reconstruction from the Almagest[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoffmann2017-45)

Delambre in his *Histoire de l'Astronomie Ancienne* (1817) concluded that Hipparchus knew and used the [equatorial coordinate system](/wiki/Equatorial_coordinate_system "Equatorial coordinate system"), a conclusion challenged by [Otto Neugebauer](/wiki/Otto_Neugebauer "Otto Neugebauer") in his *History of Ancient Mathematical Astronomy* (1975). Hipparchus seems to have used a mix of [ecliptic coordinates](/wiki/Ecliptic_coordinate_system "Ecliptic coordinate system") and [equatorial coordinates](/wiki/Equatorial_coordinate_system "Equatorial coordinate system"): in his commentary on Eudoxus he provides stars' polar distance (equivalent to the [declination](/wiki/Declination "Declination") in the equatorial system), right ascension (equatorial), longitude (ecliptic), polar longitude (hybrid), but not celestial latitude. This opinion was confirmed by the careful investigation of Hoffmann[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoffmann2017-45) who independently studied the material, potential sources, techniques and results of Hipparchus and reconstructed his celestial globe and its making.

As with most of his work, Hipparchus's star catalog was adopted and perhaps expanded by Ptolemy, who has (since Brahe in 1598) been accused by some[[49]](#cite_note-51) of fraud for stating (*Syntaxis*, book 7, chapter 4) that he observed all 1025 stars—critics claim that, for almost every star, he used Hipparchus's data and precessed it to his own epoch 2+2⁄3 centuries later by adding 2°40′ to the longitude, using an erroneously small precession constant of 1° per century. This claim is highly exaggerated because it applies modern standards of citation to an ancient author. True is only that "the ancient star catalogue" that was initiated by Hipparchus in the second century BC, was reworked and improved multiple times in the 265 years to the Almagest (which is good scientific practise even today).[[50]](#cite_note-hoffmann2018-52) Although the Almagest star catalogue is based upon Hipparchus's, it is not only a blind copy but enriched, enhanced, and thus (at least partially) re-observed.[[16]](#cite_note-hoffman-befunde-17)

### Celestial globe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hipparchus&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Celestial globe")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/HipparchsGlobus_smh2017.png/250px-HipparchsGlobus_smh2017.png)](/wiki/File:HipparchsGlobus_smh2017.png)

Reconstruction of Hipparchus's celestial globe according to ancient descriptions and the data in manuscripts by his hand (excellence cluster TOPOI, Berlin, 2015 - published in Hoffmann (2017)[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoffmann2017-45)).

Hipparchus's celestial globe was an instrument similar to modern electronic computers.[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoffmann2017-45) He used it to determine risings, settings and culminations (cf. also Almagest, book VIII, chapter 3). Therefore, his globe was mounted in a horizontal plane and had a meridian ring with a scale. In combination with a grid that divided the celestial equator into 24 hour lines (longitudes equalling our right ascension hours) the instrument allowed him to determine the hours. The ecliptic was marked and divided in 12 sections of equal length (the "signs", which he called *zodion* or *dodekatemoria* in order to distinguish them from constellations (*astron*). The globe was virtually reconstructed by a historian of science.

### Arguments for and against Hipparchus's star catalog in the Almagest

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hipparchus&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Arguments for and against Hipparchus's star catalog in the Almagest")]

For:

* common errors in the reconstructed Hipparchian star catalogue and the Almagest suggest a direct transfer without re-observation within 265 years. There are 18 stars with common errors - for the other ~800 stars, the errors are not extant or within the error ellipse. That means, no further statement is allowed on these hundreds of stars.
* further statistical arguments

Against:

* Unlike Ptolemy, Hipparchus did not use ecliptic coordinates to describe stellar positions.
* Hipparchus's catalogue is reported in Roman times to have enlisted about 850 stars but Ptolemy's catalogue has 1025 stars. Thus, somebody has added further entries.
* There are stars cited in the Almagest from Hipparchus that are missing in the Almagest star catalogue. Thus, by all the reworking within scientific progress in 265 years, not all of Hipparchus's stars made it into the Almagest version of the star catalogue.

Conclusion: Hipparchus's star catalogue is one of the sources of the Almagest star catalogue but not the only source.[[50]](#cite_note-hoffmann2018-52)

## Precession of the equinoxes (146–127 BC)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hipparchus&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Precession of the equinoxes (146–127 BC)")]

See also: [Precession (astronomy)](/wiki/Precession_(astronomy) "Precession (astronomy)")

Hipparchus is generally recognized as discoverer of the [precession](/wiki/Precession "Precession") of the [equinoxes](/wiki/Equinox "Equinox") in 127 BC.[[51]](#cite_note-jones2010-53) His two books on precession, *On the Displacement of the Solstitial and Equinoctial Points* and *On the Length of the Year*, are both mentioned in the *[Almagest](/wiki/Almagest "Almagest")* of Claudius [Ptolemy](/wiki/Ptolemy "Ptolemy"). According to Ptolemy, Hipparchus measured the longitude of [Spica](/wiki/Spica "Spica") and [Regulus](/wiki/Regulus "Regulus") and other bright stars. Comparing his measurements with data from his predecessors, [Timocharis](/wiki/Timocharis "Timocharis") and [Aristillus](/wiki/Aristillus "Aristillus"), he concluded that Spica had moved 2° relative to the [autumnal equinox](/wiki/September_equinox "September equinox"). He also compared the lengths of the [tropical year](/wiki/Tropical_year "Tropical year") (the time it takes the Sun to return to an equinox) and the [sidereal year](/wiki/Sidereal_year "Sidereal year") (the time it takes the Sun to return to a fixed star), and found a slight discrepancy. Hipparchus concluded that the equinoxes were moving ("precessing") through the zodiac, and that the rate of precession was not less than 1° in a century.

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hipparchus&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Geography")]

Hipparchus's treatise *Against the Geography of Eratosthenes* in three books is not preserved.[[52]](#cite_note-54)
Most of our knowledge of it comes from [Strabo](/wiki/Strabo "Strabo"), according to whom Hipparchus thoroughly and often unfairly criticized [Eratosthenes](/wiki/Eratosthenes "Eratosthenes"), mainly for internal contradictions and inaccuracy in determining positions of geographical localities. Hipparchus insists that a geographic map must be based only on astronomical measurements of [latitudes and longitudes](/wiki/Latitude_and_longitude "Latitude and longitude") and [triangulation](/wiki/Triangulation "Triangulation") for finding unknown distances.
In geographic theory and methods Hipparchus introduced three main innovations.[[53]](#cite_note-55)

He was the first to use the [grade grid](/wiki/Geographic_coordinate_system "Geographic coordinate system"), to determine [geographic latitude](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude") from star observations, and not only from the Sun's altitude, a method known long before him, and to suggest that [geographic longitude](/wiki/Longitude "Longitude") could be determined by means of simultaneous observations of lunar eclipses in distant places. In the practical part of his work, the so-called "table of [climata](/wiki/Clime "Clime")", Hipparchus listed latitudes for several tens of localities. In particular, he improved Eratosthenes's values for the latitudes of [Athens](/wiki/Athens "Athens"), [Sicily](/wiki/Sicily "Sicily"), and [southern extremity of India](/wiki/Comorin "Comorin").[[54]](#cite_note-shcheglov2010-56)[[55]](#cite_note-shcheglov2006-57)[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShcheglov2007-58) In calculating latitudes of [climata](/wiki/Clime "Clime") (latitudes correlated with the length of the longest solstitial day), Hipparchus used an unexpectedly accurate value for [the obliquity of the ecliptic](/wiki/Axial_tilt "Axial tilt"), 23°40′ (the actual value in the second half of the second century BC was approximately 23°43'), whereas all other ancient authors knew only a roughly rounded value 24°, and even [Ptolemy](/wiki/Ptolemy "Ptolemy") used a less accurate value, 23°51′.[[57]](#cite_note-59)

Hipparchus opposed the view generally accepted in the [Hellenistic period](/wiki/Hellenistic_period "Hellenistic period") that the [Atlantic](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean") and [Indian Oceans](/wiki/Indian_Ocean "Indian Ocean") and the [Caspian Sea](/wiki/Caspian_Sea "Caspian Sea") are parts of a single ocean. At the same time he extends the limits of the [oikoumene](/wiki/Ecumene "Ecumene"), i.e. the inhabited part of the land, up to the [equator](/wiki/Equator "Equator") and the [Arctic Circle](/wiki/Arctic_Circle "Arctic Circle").[[58]](#cite_note-shcheglov2007-thule-60) Hipparchus's ideas found their reflection in the *[Geography](/wiki/Geography_(Ptolemy) "Geography (Ptolemy)")* of [Ptolemy](/wiki/Ptolemy "Ptolemy"). In essence, Ptolemy's work is an extended attempt to realize Hipparchus's vision of what geography ought to be.

## Other work

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hipparchus&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Other work")]

Hipparchus wrote on combinatorial arithmetic and on the motion of falling bodies, though these works are now lost. In his treatment of motion he departed from [Aristotelian physics](/wiki/Aristotelian_physics "Aristotelian physics") and followed [Strato of Lampsacus](/wiki/Strato_of_Lampsacus "Strato of Lampsacus") in explaining falling bodies through an early form of [impetus theory](/wiki/Theory_of_impetus "Theory of impetus") rather than natural-place mechanics. Hipparchus also wrote lost works on [optics](/wiki/Optics "Optics"), in which he developed an [atomist](/wiki/Atomism "Atomism") theory of [light](/wiki/Light "Light"), though this theory was not universally accepted.[[59]](#cite_note-61)

## Modern speculation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hipparchus&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Modern speculation")]

Hipparchus was in the international news in 2005, when it was again proposed (as in 1898) that the data on the [celestial globe](/wiki/Celestial_globe "Celestial globe") of Hipparchus or in his star catalog may have been preserved in the only surviving large ancient celestial globe which depicts the constellations with moderate accuracy, the globe carried by the [Farnese Atlas](/wiki/Farnese_Atlas "Farnese Atlas").[[60]](#cite_note-62)[[61]](#cite_note-63) Evidence suggests that the Farnese globe may show constellations in the Aratean tradition and deviate from the constellations used by Hipparchus.[[43]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHoffmann2017-45)

A line in Plutarch's *Table Talk* states that Hipparchus counted 103,049 compound propositions that can be formed from ten simple propositions. 103,049 is the tenth [Schröder–Hipparchus number](/wiki/Schr%C3%B6der%E2%80%93Hipparchus_number "Schröder–Hipparchus number"), which counts the number of ways of adding one or more pairs of parentheses around consecutive subsequences of two or more items in any sequence of ten symbols. This has led to speculation that Hipparchus knew about [enumerative combinatorics](/wiki/Enumerative_combinatorics "Enumerative combinatorics"), a field of mathematics that developed independently in modern mathematics.[[62]](#cite_note-stanley1997-64)[[63]](#cite_note-acerbi2003-65)

Hipparchus was suggested in a 2013 paper to have accidentally observed the planet [Uranus](/wiki/Uranus "Uranus") in 128 BC and catalogued it as a star, over a millennium and a half before its formal discovery in 1781.[[64]](#cite_note-66)

## Legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hipparchus&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Legacy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/Hipparcos-testing-estec.jpg/250px-Hipparcos-testing-estec.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hipparcos-testing-estec.jpg)

*Hipparcos* satellite in the Large Solar Simulator, ESTEC, February 1988

Hipparchus may be depicted opposite [Ptolemy](/wiki/Ptolemy "Ptolemy") in [Raphael](/wiki/Raphael "Raphael")'s 1509–1511 painting *[The School of Athens](/wiki/The_School_of_Athens "The School of Athens")*, although this figure is usually identified as [Zoroaster](/wiki/Zoroaster "Zoroaster").[[36]](#cite_note-swerdlow1992-38)

The formal name for the [ESA](/wiki/European_Space_Agency "European Space Agency")'s [Hipparcos Space Astrometry Mission](/wiki/Hipparcos "Hipparcos") is High Precision Parallax Collecting Satellite, making a [backronym](/wiki/Backronym "Backronym"), HiPParCoS, that echoes and commemorates the name of Hipparchus.

The lunar crater [Hipparchus](/wiki/Hipparchus_(lunar_crater) "Hipparchus (lunar crater)"), the Martian crater [Hipparchus](/wiki/Hipparchus_(Martian_crater) "Hipparchus (Martian crater)"), and the [asteroid](/wiki/Asteroid "Asteroid") [4000 Hipparchus](/wiki/4000_Hipparchus "4000 Hipparchus") are named after him.

He was inducted into the [International Space Hall of Fame](/wiki/International_Space_Hall_of_Fame "International Space Hall of Fame") in 2004.[[65]](#cite_note-ep4-67)

[Jean Baptiste Joseph Delambre](/wiki/Jean_Baptiste_Joseph_Delambre "Jean Baptiste Joseph Delambre"), historian of astronomy, mathematical astronomer and director of the [Paris Observatory](/wiki/Paris_Observatory "Paris Observatory"), in his history of astronomy in the 18th century (1821), considered Hipparchus along with [Johannes Kepler](/wiki/Johannes_Kepler "Johannes Kepler") and [James Bradley](/wiki/James_Bradley "James Bradley") the greatest astronomers of all time.[[66]](#cite_note-delambre1827-68)

The *[Astronomers Monument](/wiki/Astronomers_Monument "Astronomers Monument")* at the [Griffith Observatory](/wiki/Griffith_Observatory "Griffith Observatory") in Los Angeles, California, United States features a relief of Hipparchus as one of six of the greatest astronomers of all time and the only one from Antiquity.[[67]](#cite_note-69)

[Johannes Kepler](/wiki/Johannes_Kepler "Johannes Kepler") had great respect for [Tycho Brahe](/wiki/Tycho_Brahe "Tycho Brahe")'s methods and the accuracy of his observations, and considered him to be the new Hipparchus, who would provide the foundation for a restoration of the science of astronomy.[[68]](#cite_note-70)

From Poniatowski (1833), p. 52: "... Dans le champ de cette pierre on voit une étoile et en beaux caractères le nom du sujet. *Améthyste*." [In the field of this stone we see a star and in beautiful characters the name of the subject. *Amethyst*.][[69]](#cite_note-gem-71)This engraving was used for the title page of [William Henry Smyth](/wiki/William_Henry_Smyth "William Henry Smyth")'s 1844 book, as suggested by an 1842 letter Smyth sent to the *National Institute for the Promotion of Science*, which described "the head of Hipparchus, from the Poniatowski-gem, intended as a vignette illustration of his work".[[70]](#cite_note-smyth-72) The engraving has subsequently been repeatedly copied and re-used as a representation of Hipparchus, for instance in a 1965 Greek [postage stamp](/wiki/Postage_stamp "Postage stamp") commemorating the [Eugenides Planetarium](/wiki/Eugenides_Foundation "Eugenides Foundation") in Athens.[[71]](#cite_note-wilson1989-73)

On the whole, it would seem that the value of his testimony has been greatly exaggerated, and its unsatisfactory nature on so many points insufficiently emphasized. At any rate, the 'instrument' he sent to Paeonius was either a modified astrolabic clock of the Vitruvian type or a simple celestial map, and not a planispheric astrolabe. Furthermore, on the evidence available we are not, in my opinion, justified in attributing to Hipparchus a knowledge of either stereographic projection or the planispheric astrolabe."
